source.title: Changing the game: exploring infants' participation in early play routines

confidence_rationale: Provides direct empirical evidence for continuous frame monitoring at 3 months of age -- infants track multimodal game structure moment-by-moment and respond with reduced engagement when structure is altered mid-game. This is the earliest developmental evidence for continuous frame monitoring rather than discrete boundary detection. Transfer risk is moderate because 3-month-old mother-infant games are far from peer rule games. Mapping fidelity for Q-035 is high in terms of directionality (evidence for ongoing monitoring) but involves a large developmental step.
failure_signatures: Study uses 3-month-old infants in mother-infant dyads -- this is sensorimotor play, not games-with-rules, making the direct REE mapping indirect.
Game structure alteration was adult-imposed (researcher removed sound or gesture) rather than partner-generated violation; does not test bilateral enforcement.
Effects are on engagement metrics (limb movements, gaze) not protest or repair -- normative enforcement is not present at this age.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Three-month-old infants show temporally precise, reduced engagement when a familiar play routine's multimodal structure is altered (removing sound or gesture mid-game), suggesting ongoing tracking of game structure rather than discrete boundary recognition.
mapping.ree_translation: The earliest human data point for continuous frame monitoring. If 3-month-olds track the internal structure of play routines moment-by-moment, the developmental lineage suggests continuous monitoring is phylogenetically primitive -- not an achievement of games-with-rules specifically. This refines Q-035: continuous monitoring may be present throughout play type development, not a novel requirement at the games-with-rules stage. What games-with-rules add is the normative complexity of what is being monitored (rules, strategy, fairness), not the monitoring architecture itself.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Large developmental gap: 3-month sensorimotor games to peer games-with-rules spans ~4 years. The monitoring mechanism at 3 months is dyadic, adult-driven, and pre-normative. Transfer to peer-generated, rule-governed frames requires substantial interpretive extrapolation.
mapping.source_context: N=20 three-month-old infants, structured mother-infant play, Southampton/Rome lab.

confidence_components.notes: Good empirical design; Frontiers in Psychology is a reputable venue. Mapping fidelity reduced by large developmental gap from infant dyadic games to peer rule games.
